title: Friday Sermon 1 February 2002
description:
The sermon is on the Divine attribute of al-Mumin (The Bestower of Peace) expounding the subject with reference to 'The Holy Quran' and the Ahadith:

Mahmud ibn Labīd (Allah be pleased with him) narrates that Messenger of Allah (peace and blessings be on him) said: Allah protects His servants from the greed and desire of this world  in the manner that one  protects an ailing person from whatever is detrimental to their  health.

(Ahmad ibn Hanbal.  'Musnad Ahmad ibn Hanbal').

Narrated Anas: The Prophetﷺ said ‘None of you will have faith till he wishes for his  brother what he likes for himself.

(Muhammad ibn Ismail al-Bukhari.  'Sahih Bukhari').

Another meaning of Mumin is one who gives peace or security and receives peace or security:

The Holy Prophet (peace and blessings be on him) said: The stars are a source of peace for the sky and I am a source of peace for my Companions, my Companions are a source of peace for this Ummah and when these sources of peace will be taken away, a calamity will befall on each recipient of peace - a calamity that they are warned about.

(Ibid).

Abu Saeed al-Khudri narrated that the Messenger of Allahﷺ said: Whoever eats pure and wholesome food and acts in accordance with the Sunnah [Prophetic practice], and people are safe from his harm, he will enter Paradise.  So a man said ‘O Messenger of Allah!  This is the case with many people today.’  He replied: It shall be so for (a few) generations after me.

(Abu Isa Muhammad ibn Isa al-Tirmidhi.  'Jami al-Tirmidhi'.  English: Abu Khaliyl.  6 Vols, Darussalam, 2007. 4: 511).

There is the prominent saying of the Holy Prophet (peace and blessings be on him) that he made during his migration from Mecca to Medina in the cave in reply to Hazrat Abu Bakr's (may Allah be pleased him) sense of trepidation at the possibility of being found by the Meccans: O Abu Bakr!  What do you make of these two, with whom the third is God?

(Bukhari).

At the battle of Badr, the Holy Prophet (peace and blessings be on him) stood by a pit that had bodies of the non-believers and remarked that now these dead bodies knew that whatever he had told them was the truth.

(Ibn Hanbal).

Citing from the writings of the Promised Messiah (on whom be peace): 

… when the love of Allah and accord with Allah completely saturate his nature, so much so that God becomes his ears by which he hears, his eyes with which he sees, his hands with which he grips, and his feet with which he walks, then no trace of darkness remains in him, and he becomes secure against every danger.  It is this stage which is indicated in Allah’s words:

Those who believe and debase not their belief with injustice are the ones who shall be            secure and who are rightly guide.*

(Hazrat Mirza Ghulam Ahmad.  'Barāhīn -  E - Aḥmadiyya: iv'.  5 vols, (Riyaz e Hind, 1884).  'Ruhani Khazain'.  602f11, 603f11).

The Promised Messiah (on whom be peace) enjoined believers never to take sense of security for granted and always engage in seeking forgiveness from Allah.

(Ahmad.  27 February 1904.  'Malfuzat: iii'.  594, 595).

There are a few revelations of the Promised Messiah (on whom be peace):

… There was a revelation in Urdu but it was a long one and the exact words have escaped my  memory.  The substance of it was: Deliverance is through faith.

(Ahmad.  18 October 1902.  'Tadhkirah'.  356.  English: Islam International Publications Ltd, 2018.  590.

* 'The Holy Quran'.  Al Anam [The Livestock]: 83.

The text https://www.alislam.org/urdu/sermon/FST20020201-UR.pdf
